video_summary: This video comprehensively introduces CI/CD (Continuous Integration Continuous Delivery), explaining its core concepts, necessity by contrasting it with manual development problems, detailing stages like build, test, and deployment, distinguishing between continuous delivery and continuous deployment, outlining popular CI/CD tools, and describing various deployment strategies.

concepts.colloquial_context: core concept of DevOps
concepts.description: A set of practices that combines software development (Dev) and IT operations (Ops), of which CI/CD is a core component.

concepts.colloquial_context: what exactly is CICD
concepts.description: An automated approach to software development that involves continuously integrating code changes, building and testing them, and then preparing them for release or deployment. It is the automation of the software development lifecycle steps.

concepts.colloquial_context: software development lifecycle
concepts.description: The entire process that changes in any application go through, from development to deployment, involving steps like coding, building, testing, and releasing.

concepts.colloquial_context: develop any feature that we have
concepts.description: The initial stage of the SDLC where new functionalities or improvements are designed and coded by developers.

concepts.colloquial_context: commit and push the changes onto our own branch... integrate their own branch changes with the main branch... using a version control system Git
concepts.description: A system (like Git) used to manage changes to source code over time, allowing multiple developers to work concurrently, manage different code branches, and merge their contributions.

concepts.colloquial_context: then comes our build stage. Build stage is building deployable artifacts from the application code.
concepts.description: The process of compiling source code, along with its dependencies and libraries, into deployable artifacts such as binary files, executable files, or packages (e.g., .apk for Android).

concepts.colloquial_context: After the build, we generally test our application. There are many tests included in testing.
concepts.description: The process of verifying an application's functionality and quality, including unit tests (by developers), integration tests (component interaction), and regression tests (new changes don't break old features).

concepts.colloquial_context: we generally go to the release step. Release means making the entire application available to the end users to use it.
concepts.description: The phase in the SDLC where the application, after successful testing, is made available for use by end-users.

concepts.colloquial_context: deploying the application. Deployment means making any application, any code, any software available in a particular environment.
concepts.description: The act of making an application, code, or software accessible within a specific environment. Common environments include development (dev), staging (pre-production), and production (prod) for end-users.

concepts.colloquial_context: Before CICD, in the early days of software development, the whole process was manual.
concepts.description: The traditional software development approach where build, test, and deployment steps were performed manually, leading to human errors, time consumption, and inefficiencies.

concepts.colloquial_context: continuous integration, in which we automate the build and test steps of our development lifecycle.
concepts.description: The practice of automating the build and test stages of the software development lifecycle, triggered by every code change to quickly detect and address integration issues.

concepts.colloquial_context: when we automate our release or deployment step, we call it continuous delivery.
concepts.description: The practice of automating the release and deployment stages, ensuring the application is always in a deployable state. It typically involves a manual approval step before deployment to production.

concepts.colloquial_context: without any manual approval, automatic deployment is done on production. there we are using continuous deployment in CI-CD.
concepts.description: An advanced form of Continuous Delivery where applications are automatically deployed to the production environment after passing all automated tests, without any manual intervention or approval.

concepts.colloquial_context: we will give rise to a problem called integration hell.
concepts.description: A problem arising from manual integration processes, where merging code from multiple developers at once leads to frequent conflicts and errors, making the integration process extremely difficult and time-consuming.

concepts.colloquial_context: we will get another problem which is of infrequent release.
concepts.description: A consequence of manual software development processes where code freezes, time-consuming manual testing, and manual deployment lead to long intervals between new software releases for users.

concepts.colloquial_context: how do we implement CICD pipelines? we have to write automation scripts for that.
concepts.description: An automated workflow, typically defined by scripts (e.g., YAML files), that orchestrates the stages of CI/CD, from code commit to deployment, ensuring a streamlined and continuous process.

concepts.colloquial_context: .yaml, which stands for Yet Another Markup Language, in that we have to write some instructions.
concepts.description: A human-friendly data serialization standard, commonly used for configuration files and defining instructions for CI/CD pipelines.

concepts.colloquial_context: multiple tools which are available to us. We have GitHub Actions, Jenkins, CircleCI, TravisCI, GitLab CI, CID, Bamboo.
concepts.description: Software platforms and applications that facilitate the implementation and management of CI/CD pipelines, such as GitHub Actions and Jenkins.

concepts.colloquial_context: we have different deployment strategies with our CI-CD which we use that we have almost zero downtime.
concepts.description: Methods used in CI/CD to deploy new versions of applications efficiently, aiming for minimal to zero downtime and providing mechanisms for quick rollback in case of issues.

concepts.colloquial_context: blue-green deployment. Blue-green deployment strategy says that we will create two identical but separate environments for our application.
concepts.description: A deployment strategy using two identical, separate environments: 'Blue' for the current production version and 'Green' for the new version. All traffic is switched from Blue to Green; if problems arise, traffic can be instantly reverted to Blue.

concepts.colloquial_context: In canary deployment, what we do is that we do not redirect all our traffic to the new version. Initially, we redirect a little traffic to our new version.
concepts.description: A deployment strategy where a new version is gradually rolled out to a small subset of users first. If stable, more users are progressively directed to the new version, allowing for real-world testing with minimal impact.

concepts.colloquial_context: we have a load balancer in any organization, which is connected to different instances.
concepts.description: A component that distributes incoming network traffic across multiple servers or instances, preventing overload on any single server and improving overall application availability and responsiveness.

concepts.colloquial_context: In rolling deployment, we have a single environment that we maintain. But generally, we have a load balancer... initially, it can be a single instance or a group of instances on which we deploy our new version.
concepts.description: A deployment strategy where new versions are deployed incrementally to a subset of instances within an environment. A load balancer directs traffic, allowing for gradual updates and testing without taking the entire application offline.

concepts.colloquial_context: integrate tools Docker and Kubernetes with this whole flow, then it makes our work much easier in large organizations.
concepts.description: Containerization (Docker) and container orchestration (Kubernetes) tools that are commonly integrated with CI/CD workflows to enhance portability, scalability, and management of applications, especially in large organizations.
